charter_ref: FRONTIER_CARTOGRAPHY.md §4 WS4 — effectivization hunt (empirical lower fences for ineffective thresholds; exemplar: certificates/erdos-13)
method: Three thematic hunting slices (density/AP/sumset · primes/divisibility/Egyptian/digits · graph/Ramsey/extremal). Every candidate was gated on an EFFECTIVIZATION CHECK — a literature search for a later effective bound — before grading; a candidate with a published effective bound is recorded as dead, not discarded, so no one re-hunts it.
status_note: Shortlist entries are HUNT RESULTS, conjecture-grade research directions — not claims. A fence table for any of them requires: re-verifying the ineffectivity at build time (the frontier moves), a dependency-free per-N verifier validated on known values, and a verified-up-to-N certificate. The trap-check killed 5 of 9 hunted candidates — the gate is the product.

plausible.name: Multidimensional Szemerédi (Furstenberg–Katznelson): the 2×2 axis-aligned square in [N]²
plausible.erdos_or_source: No exact erdosproblems.com number (the 1-D case is #139 r_k(N)=o(N), effective; #142 asks for its asymptotics). Source theorem: Furstenberg–Katznelson, 'An ergodic Szemerédi theorem for commuting transformations', J. Analyse Math. 34 (1978) 275–291. Corners lineage: Ajtai–Szemerédi; effective 2-D corners = Shkredov 2006.
plausible.ineffective_theorem: For any δ>0 and all sufficiently large N, every A ⊆ {1,…,N}² with |A| ≥ δN² contains four points {(x,y),(x+d,y),(x,y+d),(x+d,y+d)} with d ≥ 1 (a homothetic axis-aligned square). Equivalently D(N) := max square-free subset of [N]² satisfies D(N) = o(N²). This is the F–K multidimensional Szemerédi theorem instantiated at F = {(0,0),(1,0),(0,1),(1,1)} in ℤ².
plausible.why_ineffective: Proved only via the Furstenberg correspondence principle + ergodic multiple recurrence; the ergodic/compactness limit yields no rate and hence no computable N₀(δ). The ONLY genuinely-multidimensional configuration with effective bounds is the 3-point corner (Shkredov 2006, |A| = O(N²/(loglogN)^c)); corner-effectivity does NOT transfer to the square, because corner-free ⊆ square-free (wrong direction — a square-free set can be far larger than a corner-free one), so the square needs the full 4-point theorem, which is ineffective.
plausible.effectivization_check: Searched 'corners theorem effective bounds Shkredov / multidimensional four-point configuration bounds 2024–2026' and the Peluse–Prendiville effectivization line (arXiv 2407.08338 'Bounds in a popular multidimensional nonlinear Roth theorem', arXiv 2407.08637, Israel J. Math. distinct-degree multidim). Result: 2-D corners effective; distinct-degree multidim polynomial cases recently effectivized; but four-point / same-degree multidim configs (the square) remain WITHOUT effective bounds — explicitly, 'more than fifteen years later, no reasonable bounds are known for sets lacking any multidimensional four-point configuration'. NOT dead.
plausible.finite_quantity: D(N) = max |A ⊆ [N]²| with no axis-aligned square {(x,y),(x+d,y),(x,y+d),(x+d,y+d)}, d ≥ 1. Fence: the largest N in the computed range with D(N) ≥ ⌈cN²⌉ for a pinned density threshold c (e.g. c = 1/2). Because D(N)=o(N²) is ineffective, this last-N is finite but its location is untouched by any theorem — an empirical lower fence for the F–K threshold.
plausible.verifier_sketch: Certificate ships, per N, a claimed extremal square-free set and the value D(N). Verifier (a) checks the set is square-free by scanning all (x,y,d) — O(N³), pure Python, dependency-free; (b) certifies optimality: a dependency-free branch-and-bound re-search (feasible to small N) or, for larger N, an ILP/CP-SAT optimality certificate. Negative control: a set with one planted square must fail (a). Same structural shape as certificates/erdos-13/verify.py, lifted to 2-D.
plausible.fence_feasibility: N² binary variables, ≈N³/3 four-clause 'not-all-present' constraints; the exact value is a maximum-independent-set-in-a-4-uniform-hypergraph problem. Pure-Python dependency-free B&B: exact to N ≈ 15–18 (minutes). OR-Tools CP-SAT with a proof of optimality: N ≈ 30–40 (minutes–hours on a laptop, adds a solver dependency). Beyond ~40 the 2-D domain (N² vertices) makes it hard. Cost driver is the quadratic vertex count.
plausible.slice: density / arithmetic-progression / sumset (Behrend-range, sum-free sets, Sárközy-type divisibility-in-sumsets, covering congruences)

plausible.name: Polynomial Szemerédi (Bergelson–Leibman): a 1-D configuration on the ineffective side of the Peluse–Prendiville frontier
plausible.erdos_or_source: No exact erdosproblems.com number (relatives: #139/#142, the linear 1-D case, effective). Source theorem: Bergelson–Leibman, 'Polynomial extensions of van der Waerden's and Szemerédi's theorems', J. Amer. Math. Soc. 9 (1996) 725–753.
plausible.ineffective_theorem: Fix polynomials p₁,…,p_k ∈ ℤ[d] with p_i(0)=0. For any δ>0 and all large N, every A ⊆ {1,…,N} with |A| ≥ δN contains {x, x+p₁(d),…,x+p_k(d)} with d ≥ 1. Equivalently the max avoiding subset P(N) = o(N). Instantiate at a config NOT covered by current effective results (same-degree non-homogeneous multi-nonlinear, or otherwise past the frontier).
plausible.why_ineffective: Furstenberg correspondence + ergodic PET induction give no rate. Effective bounds are currently known ONLY for (i) distinct-degree configs (Peluse 2019, Forum Math. Pi 'Bounds for sets with no polynomial progressions'; Peluse–Prendiville, x,x+y,x+y²) and (ii) homogeneous same-degree configs (Peluse–Prendiville, arXiv 1409.8234). The general Bergelson–Leibman theorem — reasonable bounds for arbitrary configs — is flagged in the literature as 'a major open problem'.
plausible.effectivization_check: Searched Peluse / Prendiville / Leng–Sah–Sawhney 2018–2026. Distinct-degree and homogeneous same-degree → EFFECTIVE (those specific configs are DEAD). Same-degree non-homogeneous / higher-complexity → still ineffective. IMPORTANT GATE: because the frontier is closing fast, any specific config must be re-checked against the current literature before a fence claim — this config-selection gate is the candidate's fragility, not the compute.
plausible.finite_quantity: P(N) = max |A ⊆ [N]| avoiding the chosen polynomial config over all (x,d) with the pattern inside [N]. Fence: last N in range with P(N) ≥ ⌈cN⌉ for a pinned c. P(N)=o(N) ineffective ⇒ finite-but-unknown last-N.
plausible.verifier_sketch: Ship claimed extremal set + P(N); verifier checks avoidance by scanning all (x,d) — O(N²), dependency-free; certifies optimality by dependency-free B&B (to N ≈ 40–60) or ILP (to N ≈ 200+). Negative control: a set with one planted pattern must fail the avoidance check.
plausible.fence_feasibility: 1-D ⇒ cheap: N binary variables, O(N²) 'not-all-present' constraints. Dependency-free B&B exact to N ≈ 50; ILP/CP-SAT to N ≈ 200+ (minutes). Compute is not the bottleneck — the literature gate on 'which config is still ineffective' is.
plausible.slice: density / arithmetic-progression / sumset (Behrend-range, sum-free sets, Sárközy-type divisibility-in-sumsets, covering congruences)

plausible.name: Block complexity of the base-b expansion of an algebraic irrational (Adamczewski–Bugeaud)
plausible.erdos_or_source: No Erdős number; source = B. Adamczewski & Y. Bugeaud, 'On the complexity of algebraic numbers I. Expansions in integer bases,' Annals of Mathematics 165 (2007), 547–565 (arXiv:math/0511674). The strongest genuinely-ineffective, still-un-effectivized statement I found in the whole slice.
plausible.ineffective_theorem: For every real algebraic irrational ξ and every integer base b ≥ 2, the base-b block complexity p(n) = p_{ξ,b}(n) (the number of DISTINCT length-n factors occurring in the base-b digit string of ξ) satisfies lim_{n→∞} p(n)/n = +∞. Equivalently, an algebraic irrational is never automatic/morphic. This strictly strengthens the effective Morse–Hedlund floor p(n) ≥ n+1 to a superlinear-for-all-large-n statement whose growth rate is where the ineffectivity lives.
plausible.why_ineffective: The proof feeds a combinatorial transcendence criterion on 'stammering' sequences into Schmidt's Subspace Theorem. The Subspace Theorem is the archetypal ineffective tool: it bounds the NUMBER of exceptional subspaces (Evertse/Schlickewei quantitative versions) but gives no bound on their height, so no n0 can be extracted at which p(n)/n first exceeds a prescribed target R. Baker's linear-forms-in-logs machinery — the usual source of effectivity in this slice — does not apply to the Subspace Theorem.
plausible.effectivization_check: Searched for a later effective version: none exists. Effectivizing Roth/Schmidt is a central, famously-open problem in Diophantine approximation; the quantitative Subspace Theorem (Evertse–Schlickewei–Schmidt, Faltings) counts subspaces but stays height-ineffective, so the complexity rate remains ineffective. Trap (a) NOT triggered — genuinely un-effectivized. (Downstream sharpenings — Bugeaud–Evertse digit-count and Adamczewski–Cassaigne–Le Gonidec bounds — inherit the same Subspace-Theorem ineffectivity.)
plausible.finite_quantity: For a fixed algebraic irrational (canonically ξ=√2, b=2), p(n) = number of distinct length-n windows in the exact base-b digit string, computed per n from N precomputed digits (N ≫ 2^n).
plausible.verifier_sketch: Dependency-free: compute √2 to N base-2 digits via a big-integer integer-sqrt of 2·2^{2N} (no floats); slide a length-n window across the first N−n+1 digits, insert each window into a hash set, report |set| = p(n); compare p(n)/n against a target R. A stranger reruns it and gets identical integers. Negative control: a periodic string must give bounded p(n).
plausible.fence_feasibility: Digits: √2 to 10^7 binary places is milliseconds; p(n) is exactly computable for n up to ~40 (window count limited by N, not by CPU) at trivial cost. THE DEFECT: for √2 (and every concrete algebraic irrational, all empirically normal) p(n) = 2^n throughout the computable range, so p(n)/n is astronomically above any target and there is NO observable exception — the empirical fence is VACUOUS. The theorem forbids a low-complexity region that no concrete number occupies. Value of the entry is as a catalogued genuinely-ineffective in-slice theorem, not as a producible fence table.
plausible.slice: primes / divisibility / Egyptian-fraction / digit problems. HONEST HARVEST: no clean strong #13-analog exists in this slice, and the reason is structural — Baker's theorem (effective linear forms in logarithms) effectivizes the overwhelming majority of "P(N) for all large N" statements about primes, divisibility, smooth numbers, and Egyptian fractions (explicit N0 falls out). Genuine ineffectivity survives almost only where the proof runs through Roth/Schmidt (rational-approximation / Subspace Theorem), which Baker's method cannot touch. But those surviving cases live in the digit slice, and their concrete finite sides empirically SATURATE (algebraic irrationals look normal), so the "empirical lower fence" is vacuous — the opposite failure mode from #13. Result: 1 plausible + 2 weak, all effectivization-checked. NEAR-MISS worth logging on the map: the Erdős–Straus–Schinzel exceptional set (which n fail m/n = 1/a+1/b+1/c) has the cheapest, most interesting finite side in the whole slice and is verified past 10^17 — but its "true for all large n" side is an OPEN CONJECTURE, not a proven ineffective theorem, so it is a WS3 verified-up-to-N ladder, NOT a WS4 fence (Vaughan 1970 gives only a density/exp(-c(log N)^{2/3}) exceptional-count theorem; recent arXiv:2511.16817 proves exceptions persist past exp(m^{1/3-ε}), itself an ineffective lower bound on the threshold). Do not promote it to WS4.

plausible.name: Erdős–Rademacher exact minimum triangle count (Lovász–Simonovits, full-density refinement)
plausible.erdos_or_source: erdosproblems.com/1010 (Erdős–Rademacher; the headline t·⌊n/2⌋ bound for the first cn edges past ⌊n²/4⌋ is SOLVED and effective — Lovász–Simonovits, Nikiforov–Khadzhiivanov). The WS4 object is the full-density refinement: Liu, Pikhurko & Staden, 'The exact minimum number of triangles in graphs of given order and size', Forum Math. Pi / Duke 2020, arXiv:1712.00633, confirming the 1975 Lovász–Simonovits conjecture; asymptotics by Razborov 2008, Nikiforov, Reiher.
plausible.ineffective_theorem: There exists n_0 such that for all n ≥ n_0 and every m ≤ (1−o(1))·C(n,2), the minimum number of triangles h(n,m) over all n-vertex, m-edge graphs equals the explicit Lovász–Simonovits construction value (a complete multipartite 'clique-stack' graph with the residual edges placed as a near-clique/complete-bipartite remainder), and the minimisers are characterised.
plausible.why_ineffective: The exact result is obtained by graphon (graph-limit) compactness plus flag-algebra stability: one passes to the limit space of graphons, argues the extremal graphon is the LS profile, and transfers back to large finite graphs by a compactness/stability step that yields existence of n_0 but no rate. This is genuine ineffectiveness (no computable n_0 at all), distinct from a tower-type regularity bound.
plausible.effectivization_check: Searched Razborov 2008, Nikiforov, Reiher, Pikhurko–Razborov (asymptotic structure), and the LPS 2020 exact paper plus follow-ups (arXiv:2311.18753, 2003.12917 stability notes): the asymptotic/structural results and the exact result carry NO effective threshold; the graph-limit proof provides none. The only effective piece is the narrow 'first cn edges' regime (Erdős/LS/Nikiforov–Khadzhiivanov), which is a different, weaker statement. No effectivization of the full-density exact threshold found — candidate is live.
plausible.finite_quantity: h(n,m) = the exact minimum number of triangles over all graphs on n vertices with exactly m edges (a 2-parameter table; per n, the whole profile m = ex(n,K_3)+1 … up to ~(1−o(1))C(n,2)).
plausible.verifier_sketch: For each candidate graph, triangle count is a trivial O(n³) evaluation; the minimum is certified either by isomorph-free exhaustive enumeration (nauty/geng at fixed n,m) or by an ILP/SAT lower-bound certificate. Compare h(n,m) against the closed-form LS construction value for that (n,m); the certificate is (a) a witness graph achieving h(n,m) and (b) an exhaustive/DRAT nonexistence proof that no m-edge graph has fewer triangles. Record the largest (n,m) at which h(n,m) < LS value — the lower fence for n_0.
plausible.fence_feasibility: Full isomorph-free enumeration (geng) is exact to n ≈ 11–12 across all m; ILP/SAT with symmetry breaking pushes individual (n,m) cells to n ≈ 16–20. Cost per cell is a bounded search comparable to (heavier than) the #13 branch-and-bound. A modest-compute fence table over n ≲ 15 is feasible; the deliverable is the last small (n,m) where the LS construction is beaten (the LS conjecture required 'n large' precisely because sporadic small-(n,m) minimisers differ, so a nontrivial fence is expected — confirming the specific exceptions is the one open verification step).
plausible.slice: graph / Ramsey-type / extremal combinatorics (regularity-lemma consequences, stability-method theorems, hypergraph removal consequences)

plausible.name: Polynomial Szemerédi / polynomial van der Waerden (Bergelson–Leibman), general non-effectivized pattern
plausible.erdos_or_source: Bergelson & Leibman, 'Polynomial extensions of van der Waerden's and Szemerédi's theorems', J. AMS 1996 (Ramsey-type / density; partition-regular polynomial configurations). Ergodic-theoretic proof. Effectivization literature: Peluse 2019 (distinct degrees), Prendiville (homogeneous same degree), Peluse–Prendiville–Shao 2024 (a multidimensional distinct-degree case), Discrete Analysis 2024–2025 complexity-one bounds.
plausible.ineffective_theorem: For polynomials P_1,…,P_k ∈ ℤ[y] with P_i(0)=0: every A ⊆ [N] with |A| ≥ δN contains a nontrivial {x, x+P_1(d), …, x+P_k(d)} (d≠0) once N ≥ N_0(δ; P) (density form); equivalently every r-colouring of [N] has a monochromatic such configuration for N ≥ W_0(r; P) (partition form). Finiteness of N_0 / W_0 is guaranteed but for a genuinely mixed pattern comes with no bound.
plausible.why_ineffective: The general theorem's only proof is via the Furstenberg correspondence to a measure-preserving system + a PET-induction/ergodic-averages argument (Bergelson–Leibman), which produces no quantitative rate whatsoever — the ergodic limit object destroys all effectivity.
plausible.effectivization_check: TRAP (a) IS ACTIVELY FIRING. Effective bounds now exist for: distinct-degree progressions (Peluse 2019, doubly-exponential; 2025 polylog), homogeneous same-degree patterns like {x, x+y², x+2y²} (Prendiville, O((log log N)^{-c})), and a genuinely multidimensional distinct-degree corner (Peluse–Prendiville–Shao 2024). A viable WS4 instance must therefore use a pattern OUTSIDE all these classes — a genuinely mixed/multidimensional polynomial system the 2019–2025 wave has not reached. The general theorem remains ineffective, but pattern selection is now the crux; naive simple patterns are dead.
plausible.finite_quantity: Either r_P(N) = max size of a P-free subset of [N] (density face), or the polynomial van der Waerden number W_P(r) = least N forcing a monochromatic P-instance under any r-colouring (partition face — a clean SAT decision).
plausible.verifier_sketch: Density: r_P(N) is a maximum independent set in the hypergraph on [N] whose hyperedges are the pattern instances — solve by ILP/exact search (the pattern-instance list is enumerable in O(N²)); dependency-light, verifiable by replaying the independent-set witness + a co-certificate. Partition: W_P(r) by SAT/exhaustive colouring, exact for small r,N; the witness is a good colouring of [W−1] plus an UNSAT/DRAT proof at W.
plausible.fence_feasibility: r_P(N) reachable to N ≈ 10²–10³ (mirrors r_3(N) record computations); W_P(r) exactly for r = 2,3. Each computed value is a data point the ineffective theorem cannot predict; the largest N with r_P(N) ≥ δN is a genuine lower fence on N_0(δ). Cheapest and most #13-like finite side of the three.
plausible.slice: graph / Ramsey-type / extremal combinatorics (regularity-lemma consequences, stability-method theorems, hypergraph removal consequences)

dead_do_not_rehunt.name: Erdős #865 (Erdős–Sós pairwise-sums, 5/8 threshold) — trap-(a) illustration
dead_do_not_rehunt.erdos_or_source: erdosproblems.com/865; Choi–Erdős–Szemerédi [CES75], Erdős–Sós. Resolved: f₃(N) ≤ 5N/8 + O(1), Cipollini, 'A sharp 5/8 bound for an Erdős–Sós pairwise-sums problem', arXiv:2606.29361 (2026); reduction partially Lean-4/Mathlib formalized.
dead_do_not_rehunt.ineffective_theorem: (Erdős–Sós form) There exists C>0 such that for all large N, any A ⊆ {1,…,N} with |A| ≥ 5N/8 + C contains distinct a,b,c ∈ A with a+b, a+c, b+c all ∈ A; i.e. f₃(N) ≤ 5N/8 + C, matched by the construction [N/8,N/4]∪[N/2,N].
dead_do_not_rehunt.why_ineffective: It is NOT ineffective. The 2026 proof is a finitary 'folded additive lemma' counting/compression argument — no compactness, no Szemerédi regularity, no removal lemma — so the constant C and the 'sufficiently large N' threshold are effectively derivable in principle (the paper simply writes the constant as O(1)).
dead_do_not_rehunt.effectivization_check: This is where trap (a) FIRES. Read arXiv:2606.29361: the resolving proof is itself effective/finitary, so there is no ineffective theorem with a genuinely-unknown threshold to fence. What remains unknown is only the EXACT extremal function f₃(N) (bracketed 5N/8 ± O(1)) — a WS3 records / exact-value target, NOT a WS4 ineffectivity fence. DEAD as a WS4 candidate.
dead_do_not_rehunt.finite_quantity: f₃(N) = 1 + max |A ⊆ [N]| avoiding {distinct a,b,c with a+b,a+c,b+c ∈ A}. Cleanly and cheaply computable per N (Schur-like constraint), but its use here is a records certificate, not a fence.
dead_do_not_rehunt.verifier_sketch: Exactly the #13 pattern: recompute f₃(N) by branch-and-bound, brute-force cross-check for small N, re-verify each extremal witness, and tabulate f₃(N) vs ⌊5N/8⌋. Dependency-free.
dead_do_not_rehunt.fence_feasibility: Cheapest of the three (1-D, single-parameter): dependency-free B&B to N ≈ 80–100 in seconds–minutes. Compute is trivial; the candidate is nonetheless weak because the theorem is effective.
dead_do_not_rehunt.slice: density / arithmetic-progression / sumset (Behrend-range, sum-free sets, Sárközy-type divisibility-in-sumsets, covering congruences)

dead_do_not_rehunt.name: Erdős #283 — integers as sums of polynomial values over a unit-fraction partition
dead_do_not_rehunt.erdos_or_source: erdosproblems.com/283 (upstream status: Solved). Underlying theorems: R. L. Graham, 'A theorem on partitions,' J. Austral. Math. Soc. 3 (1963) 435–441 (case p(x)=x); M. Alekseyev, 'On partitions into squares of distinct integers whose reciprocals sum to 1' (arXiv:1801.05928, case p(x)=x²); W. van Doorn, arXiv:2502.02200 & arXiv:2502.01409 (2025, general).
dead_do_not_rehunt.ineffective_theorem: For an integer polynomial p with positive leading coefficient and no fixed prime divisor (no d≥2 with d | p(n) for all n≥1), every sufficiently large m can be written as m = p(n_1)+…+p(n_k) for some distinct positive integers n_1<…<n_k with 1 = 1/n_1+…+1/n_k. (The a-priori-tempting WS4 read: for a 'generic' high-degree p the completeness threshold m0(p) is not obviously tracked.)
dead_do_not_rehunt.why_ineffective: Would only be ineffective if the general completeness proof used a non-constructive compactness/greedy argument that discards the block-size accounting. It does not — see effectivization_check.
dead_do_not_rehunt.effectivization_check: DEAD — trap (a) fully triggered. Every instance anyone has examined is EFFECTIVE with an explicit threshold: Graham 1963 gives p(x)=x with every m ≥ 78 representable (largest exception exactly 77); Alekseyev gives p(x)=x² for all m > 8542; and decisively, van Doorn (2025) proves NEAR-OPTIMAL EXPLICIT upper bounds on the general thresholds n_α and n_{α,m} for prescribed reciprocal sums, plus computes exact answers (all α with n_α ≤ 100). The Cassels (1960)/Graham completeness lineage is constructive throughout. There is no polynomial for which the threshold is known to be ineffective.
dead_do_not_rehunt.finite_quantity: For a fixed admissible p: the finite set of non-representable m (equivalently the largest exception m*(p)); representability of any given m is decidable.
dead_do_not_rehunt.verifier_sketch: Dependency-free. Witness check: given a claimed set {n_i}, verify distinctness, Σ 1/n_i = 1 by exact rational arithmetic, and Σ p(n_i) = m. Non-representability of small m: bounded DP / exhaustive search over distinct-integer sets whose reciprocals sum to 1, tabulating attainable p-value sums; reproduces Graham's 77 as a control.
dead_do_not_rehunt.fence_feasibility: For p=x the full exception set (max 77) recomputes in seconds to m~10^4; higher-degree/shifted p push to moderate m cheaply. But since the threshold is EFFECTIVELY known for every studied case, there is no ineffective threshold to fence — this belongs in WS3 (verified-up-to-N certificate of the exception table), not WS4.
dead_do_not_rehunt.slice: primes / divisibility / Egyptian-fraction / digit problems. HONEST HARVEST: no clean strong #13-analog exists in this slice, and the reason is structural — Baker's theorem (effective linear forms in logarithms) effectivizes the overwhelming majority of "P(N) for all large N" statements about primes, divisibility, smooth numbers, and Egyptian fractions (explicit N0 falls out). Genuine ineffectivity survives almost only where the proof runs through Roth/Schmidt (rational-approximation / Subspace Theorem), which Baker's method cannot touch. But those surviving cases live in the digit slice, and their concrete finite sides empirically SATURATE (algebraic irrationals look normal), so the "empirical lower fence" is vacuous — the opposite failure mode from #13. Result: 1 plausible + 2 weak, all effectivization-checked. NEAR-MISS worth logging on the map: the Erdős–Straus–Schinzel exceptional set (which n fail m/n = 1/a+1/b+1/c) has the cheapest, most interesting finite side in the whole slice and is verified past 10^17 — but its "true for all large n" side is an OPEN CONJECTURE, not a proven ineffective theorem, so it is a WS3 verified-up-to-N ladder, NOT a WS4 fence (Vaughan 1970 gives only a density/exp(-c(log N)^{2/3}) exceptional-count theorem; recent arXiv:2511.16817 proves exceptions persist past exp(m^{1/3-ε}), itself an ineffective lower bound on the threshold). Do not promote it to WS4.

dead_do_not_rehunt.name: Consecutive-gap growth of S-smooth numbers (S-units)
dead_do_not_rehunt.erdos_or_source: Classical smooth-number theory. Ineffective heritage: D. Ridout, 'The p-adic generalization of the Thue–Siegel–Roth theorem,' Mathematika 5 (1958). Effective supersession: R. Tijdeman, 'On integers with many small prime factors,' Compositio Math. 26 (1973) and 'On the maximal distance between integers composed of small primes,' Compositio Math. 28 (1974), via Baker's linear forms in logarithms.
dead_do_not_rehunt.ineffective_theorem: Fix a finite prime set S and let a_1<a_2<… enumerate the S-smooth numbers (those with all prime factors in S). The tempting ineffective statement: for every ε>0, a_{n+1} − a_n ≫_ε a_n^{1−ε} for all sufficiently large n. Its natural pedigree is Ridout's p-adic Roth theorem, which is ineffective (finiteness of good rational/S-integer approximations with no computable n0).
dead_do_not_rehunt.why_ineffective: Roth/Ridout rational-approximation bounds give no computable threshold — the classic ineffective source. This is exactly the kind of statement one would hope survives Baker; it does not.
dead_do_not_rehunt.effectivization_check: DEAD — trap (a) triggered, and this is the instructive exhibit for the whole slice. Tijdeman used Baker's theorem to prove the EFFECTIVE lower bound a_{n+1} − a_n ≫ a_n / (log a_n)^C. Since (log a_n)^C = a_n^{o(1)}, this effective bound already implies a_{n+1} − a_n > a_n^{1−ε} effectively for EVERY fixed ε>0 — the ineffective Ridout version is entirely superseded, with a computable n0(S,ε). No ineffective residue remains at the a_n^{1−ε} scale.
dead_do_not_rehunt.finite_quantity: The consecutive gaps a_{n+1} − a_n of the S-smooth sequence, exactly enumerable.
dead_do_not_rehunt.verifier_sketch: Dependency-free Hamming-number generation: a min-heap (or multi-pointer merge) emits S-smooth numbers in order up to bound X; output the gap sequence and compare against a_n^{1−ε} or a_n/(log a_n)^C. Independent re-enumeration reproduces it byte-for-byte.
dead_do_not_rehunt.fence_feasibility: S-smooth numbers up to 10^18 for small |S| enumerate in seconds; gaps are immediate. But there is nothing to fence — the growth is effectively bounded from below by Baker, so no unknown ineffective threshold exists. Retained only as a clean 'Roth-heritage looks ineffective, Baker kills it' cautionary exhibit for the smooth-number sub-slice.
dead_do_not_rehunt.slice: primes / divisibility / Egyptian-fraction / digit problems. HONEST HARVEST: no clean strong #13-analog exists in this slice, and the reason is structural — Baker's theorem (effective linear forms in logarithms) effectivizes the overwhelming majority of "P(N) for all large N" statements about primes, divisibility, smooth numbers, and Egyptian fractions (explicit N0 falls out). Genuine ineffectivity survives almost only where the proof runs through Roth/Schmidt (rational-approximation / Subspace Theorem), which Baker's method cannot touch. But those surviving cases live in the digit slice, and their concrete finite sides empirically SATURATE (algebraic irrationals look normal), so the "empirical lower fence" is vacuous — the opposite failure mode from #13. Result: 1 plausible + 2 weak, all effectivization-checked. NEAR-MISS worth logging on the map: the Erdős–Straus–Schinzel exceptional set (which n fail m/n = 1/a+1/b+1/c) has the cheapest, most interesting finite side in the whole slice and is verified past 10^17 — but its "true for all large n" side is an OPEN CONJECTURE, not a proven ineffective theorem, so it is a WS3 verified-up-to-N ladder, NOT a WS4 fence (Vaughan 1970 gives only a density/exp(-c(log N)^{2/3}) exceptional-count theorem; recent arXiv:2511.16817 proves exceptions persist past exp(m^{1/3-ε}), itself an ineffective lower bound on the threshold). Do not promote it to WS4.

dead_do_not_rehunt.name: Exact hypergraph Turán numbers 'for large n' via the (strong) removal lemma (Pikhurko / Frankl–Füredi lineage)
dead_do_not_rehunt.erdos_or_source: Pikhurko, exact Turán numbers of expansions / 'Exact computation of the hypergraph Turán function for expanded complete 2-graphs'; Frankl–Füredi generalized-triangle results; cf. corpus walls #500 (K_4^3, 5/9), #712, #146 (Erdős–Simonovits degenerate bipartite). Illustrative of the trap-(a) boundary rather than a clean win.
dead_do_not_rehunt.ineffective_theorem: For various r-uniform F (e.g. expansions of graphs), there is n_0 with ex_r(n,F) = [explicit construction value] for all n ≥ n_0, with the extremal r-graph characterised.
dead_do_not_rehunt.why_ineffective: The stability→exact step routes through the (hyper)graph removal lemma; the removal lemma supplies no reasonable explicit constant, so the paper's n_0 is effectively unstated. Authors note explicitly that an alternative (removal-free) approach would be needed to get explicit, reasonably-sized thresholds.
dead_do_not_rehunt.effectivization_check: TRAP (a) PARTIALLY FIRES — and this is the honest weakness. The removal lemma is technically EFFECTIVE (tower/Ackermann-type via regularity, or Fox's tower-of-height-log(1/ε)), so a computable — astronomically large — n_0 can in principle be extracted. The result is ineffective only in the 'no FEASIBLE bound' sense, not the strict 'no computable bound' sense that the #13/Bedert exemplar and Candidate 1 (graph limits) satisfy. By a strict reading of the trap this candidate is dead; it is retained to mark exactly where the effective/ineffective line sits.
dead_do_not_rehunt.finite_quantity: ex_r(n,F) = max number of r-edges in an F-free r-graph on n vertices.
dead_do_not_rehunt.verifier_sketch: F-free check is cheap and exact — O(n^k) over all k-subsets (O(n⁴) for a tetrahedron-type F). The exact value needs a search: SAT decision '∃ F-free r-graph with ≥ m+1 edges' → DRAT nonexistence certificate pins ex_r(n,F)=m; compare to the construction value; record the last n where a sporadic construction beats it.
dead_do_not_rehunt.fence_feasibility: Only small n: the F-free check is trivial but the max-edge optimisation is a SAT/ILP+DRAT search that walls out quickly (n ≲ 12–15 typically). A specific F with a DOCUMENTED nontrivial small-n exception would need a targeted literature dive; without one, the fence may be trivial. Heaviest finite side of the three.
dead_do_not_rehunt.slice: graph / Ramsey-type / extremal combinatorics (regularity-lemma consequences, stability-method theorems, hypergraph removal consequences)
